narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 missense variant p.D1739G (c.5216A>G) alters an aspartic acid at position 1739 of the BRCA1 protein and has been investigated for its role in hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer. In the literature this change has been described as "cancer‑associated" based on criteria such as a polarity change, high evolutionary conservation and supportive in‑silico predictions, and it was assigned a pathogenic classification in prior reports [PMC2871096].
Clinical data from the same study show that the variant was identified among a set of unclassified variants (UVs) in the investigated families, and the authors initially labeled it as of uncertain significance in their cohort analysis [PMC2871096]. However, the same article also reports that the variant yielded high mutation probability scores—97.3% with BRCAPRO and 32.5% with Myriad II—exceeding the 20% threshold used to prioritize likely pathogenic mutations, and the authors note that it was the only UV besides c.4987‑3C>G to achieve such scores, supporting a pathogenic interpretation [PMC2871096].
Computational assessments referenced in the article indicate that the p.D1739G substitution results in a polarity change and occurs at a highly conserved residue across mammals and other species, factors that in silico models consider indicative of deleterious impact [PMC2871096]. No functional laboratory studies or population frequency data were presented for this variant in the extracted evidence.
Overall, the evidence is mixed: while the variant is classified as cancer‑associated in the literature and shows high predictive probabilities from BRCAPRO and Myriad II, the study’s own cohort initially treated it as an unclassified variant of uncertain significance. The balance of the reported data leans toward a pathogenic interpretation, but the presence of uncertainty in the cohort classification highlights the need for additional functional or segregation data to resolve the discrepancy.

As shown in Table 5, with Myriad II nine UVs and with BRCAPRO seven of the 11 UVs had mutation probabilities below 20%. The UVs: c.4987-3C>G (putative splice variant), and **c.5216A>G** in BRCA1 were the only two UVs with probability values >20% both with BRCAPRO and Myriad II.

Interestingly, from the two UVs with mutation probabilities above 20% with BRCAPRO and Myriad II, the **p.D1739G** variant in BRCA1 has been classified as cancer-associated in the literature according to criteria, such as polarity change, conservation amongst species and in silico models. Conversely, for most of those UVs with a mutation probability below 20%, evidence from other sources also argue against them being deleterious. This is the case for: p.R496C in BRCA1 and p.E462G and p.V2908G in BRCA2.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the BRCA1 p.D1739G (c.5216A>G) variant is classified as cancer‑associated (pathogenic) in the literature and shows high BRCAPRO probability, while also being listed among unclassified variants in the study cohort.
article_mentions.title: Efficiency of BRCAPRO and Myriad II mutation probability thresholds versus cancer history criteria alone for BRCA1/2 mutation detection
